title: Reading News
intro: What's topping your reading logs this month — a brand-new memoir from Michael Morpurgo, fresh chapter books for children who've outgrown the early readers, and a few dates for the diary.

items.headline: The Worst Witch — still topping your logs, on the page and on screen
items.author: Jill Murphy
items.book: A Bad Spell for the Worst Witch
items.summary: Mildred Hubble is comfortably your most-read character. Puffin keep the series fresh with bright new covers, and children who finish the books can carry straight on with the much-loved CBBC television series. A safe recommendation for any child who likes a bit of magic and mischief.

items.headline: From A Box of Socks to spooky chapter books — Amanda Brandon moves up a band
items.author: Amanda Brandon
items.summary: Amanda Brandon — whose A Box of Socks early reader is one of your most-read titles — has new chapter books out, including The Curse of Raven's Hall, an illustrated adventure about pupils trapped in a creepy old house. A natural next step for a child who's outgrown her early readers but still wants that same warmth and humour.

items.headline: The Nowhere Boy comes from a teacher who knows her readers
items.author: Sandra Glover
items.summary: Sandra Glover was a head of history before she turned to writing, and The Nowhere Boy — one of your most-read titles — was her very first novel, shortlisted for the Lancashire Children's Book of the Year. Its mix of everyday foster-family life and a mysterious boy who speaks straight into Mandy's mind is a gentle way into science fiction for confident readers.

items.headline: Biff, Chip and Kipper — a watch-alike for your earliest readers
items.author: Roderick Hunt
items.summary: The Oxford Reading Tree trio your beginner readers meet have their own screen outings — the CBeebies series Biff & Chip and the classic Magic Key animation — worth pointing families to for a little reading support at home.

items.headline: Michael Morpurgo's most personal book yet — fifty years on a Devon farm
items.author: Michael Morpurgo
items.summary: The author of Running Wild publishes Down Burrow Lane: Life and Love on the Farm on 17 September — a memoir of the fifty years he and his wife Clare spent on the Devon farm that inspired War Horse and Private Peaceful, and where they founded the Farms for City Children charity. Lovely background for a class already hooked on his animal stories.

items.headline: More gory-fun history from the Horrible Histories author
items.author: Terry Deary
items.summary: Terry Deary's Terrible True Tales bring the same grisly humour as Horrible Histories to newly-confident readers, in shorter illustrated stories. Handy for a child who loves the facts but wants a lighter read.

events.name: Summer Reading Challenge — Read to the Beat!
events.blurb: The Reading Agency's free library challenge runs 4 July to 5 September in England and Wales (from 20 June in Scotland), this year pairing stories with songs alongside Universal Music, with illustrations by Harry Woodgate. A great nudge to keep children reading over the holidays.

events.name: Beatrix Potter's 160th birthday
events.blurb: The creator of Peter Rabbit was born on 28 July 1866 — a lovely prompt for a Potter display or a read-aloud of the little tales with your youngest classes.

events.name: Roald Dahl Day
events.blurb: Marked each year on Dahl's birthday, 13 September — this year his 110th. Schools often celebrate with dressing-up, golden tickets and a chapter or two of the classics.

events.name: National Poetry Day — 'Wonder'
events.blurb: Thursday 1 October, this year on the theme of Wonder. Free classroom resources go up via the Forward Arts Foundation and the National Literacy Trust.

events.name: Michael Morpurgo's birthday
events.blurb: One of your most-read authors turns a year older on 5 October — pair it with Running Wild, or his new farm memoir Down Burrow Lane, out the month before.

events.name: World Book Day
events.blurb: The next World Book Day falls on Thursday 4 March 2027. Worth pencilling in early for £1 book tokens, dressing-up and reading-for-pleasure activities.

general.label: Reading research
general.text: Research from the University of Sussex found that just six minutes of reading can reduce stress levels by up to 68% — more than music or a walk.

general.label: Reading research
general.text: OECD analysis found that reading for pleasure is one of the biggest indicators of a child's future success — a stronger predictor than the family's wealth or background.

general.label: Did you know?
general.text: A child who reads for around 20 minutes a day meets roughly 1.8 million words a year — a big part of why regular readers pull ahead.
